edge: HARP (HAnd Reconstruction and Personalization) is a personalized hand avatar creation approach that reconstructs a faithful hand avatar from a short monocular RGB video of a human hand.

In contrast to the major trend of neural implicit representations, HARP models a hand with a mesh-based parametric hand model, a vertex displacement map, a normal map, and an albedo without any neural components.
HARP is optimized via gradient descent from a short sequence captured by a hand-held mobile phone and can be directly used in AR/VR applications with real-time rendering capability.

To enable direct usage in AR/VR applications, HARP implements a shadow-aware differentiable rendering scheme that is robust to high degree articulations and self-shadowing in hand motions, as well as challenging lighting conditions.
The learned HARP representation can be used to improve 3D hand pose estimation quality in challenging viewpoints.
HARP, with its efficient hand avatar creation and rendering capabilities, is suited for use in AR/VR applications.

Both 'novel view' and 'novel pose' are key concepts related to 3D hand pose refinement as mentioned in the context. They are essential in the process of reconstructing and synthesizing realistic hand representations for AR/VR applications.

The 'AR/VR-ready personalized hand representation' is described to have superior fidelity, showing high accuracy and detail in its representation. This high fidelity contributes to the scalability of the hand model for immersive AR/VR experiences.
Advancements in AR/VR devices are introducing a new reality where the 'physical and digital worlds merge.' This merging of realities is a significant aspect of the evolving AR/VR environment.

The 'human hand' serves as the primary interface between humans and the digital world in AR/VR environments. It plays a crucial role in facilitating interactive experiences within these digital spaces.

Research into 'hand avatar creation' has been limited despite its importance in AR/VR applications. Most existing works focus on appearance space representation but lack expressivity for non-standard textures, indicating a gap in research in this area.

The 'LISA model' utilizes an implicit function to represent hand geometry and texture color fields, distinguishing it from traditional appearance space models. However, training new identities in LISA requires specific setup and substantial computational resources.

In the fields of 'face and body avatar creation,' many works leverage implicit functions or NeRF-based volume representations for modeling. These techniques offer alternative approaches to traditional parametric models for realistic avatar creation.
The NeRF-based method, such as HumanNeRF, is mentioned as a convincing novel view synthesis, but it still shows blurry artifacts around highly articulated parts.
It is argued that democratizing hand avatar creation for AR/VR users requires a method like HARP that is accurate, scalable, robust, and efficient.
The observation is that human hands are highly articulated, and HARP is proposed as a method to create a faithful hand avatar from a short RGB video.
Learning neural representations, such as implicit texture ﬁelds, is vulnerable to over-fitting to a short monocular training sequence.
Learning neural representations, such as implicit texture ﬁelds or volume-based representations like NeRF, is vulnerable to over-fitting to a short monocular training sequence.

Learning neural representations, such as implicit texture ﬁelds or volume-based representations like NeRF, can hardly generalize well to sophisticated and dexterous hand movements, while HARP significantly improves the reconstruction quality and generate life-like renderings on novel views.

Standard explicit geometry and appearance representations together with a carefully implemented differentiable rendering scheme provide the right balance between quality, speed, and compatibility with other graphics applications.
To render the hand in the camera view, the differentiable rasterizer is utilized to determine the visible surfaces from the camera views.
The Phong model without the specular component is used to compute the appearance of the hand in the camera view.
The Phong model is used without the specular component to compute illumination at the surface point x.
The Phong model includes ambient reflection constant, diffuse reflection constant, intensities of light sources, normal at point x, and a ray toward each light source to compute illumination.
Having just one dominant light source provides a good balance between computation cost and rendering quality, assuming the closest light source produces the hard shadow.
The Phong model does not account for self-shadowing when a finger casts a shadow onto other fingers and the palm.
Visibility term is added at x with respect to light m to the diffuse component to accommodate self-shadowing scenario.
Visibility term is computed by performing a two-step rasterization of the mesh to add it to the diffuse component.
The classic idea of shadow mapping in computer graphics is integrated into the differentiable pipeline for computation.
If the light position is more than 1 m away from the hand, it is projected to 1 m distance.

To produce a softer shadow, we use percentage-closer filtering [61], which averages the visibility values of the nearby points.

To find the parameters that describe the personalized hand, we optimize the parameters using short RGB videos.
The differentiable rendering pipeline is optimized for each subject mentioned.
The joint objective consists of a geometry alignment term and an appearance term for each subject.
The geometry alignment term is a part of the joint objective E.
The appearance term encourages the same appearance as in the input images.
The goal for geometry is to match the rendered silhouette with the hand mask while satisfying 3D mesh constraints.
The geometry objective focuses on matching the rendered silhouette with the hand mask.
The silhouette difference term is the l1-difference between the hand mask and the rendered silhouette.
The geometry should also satisfy 3D mesh constraints.
The silhouette difference term is influenced by weights.
Mesh regularizations prevent the optimized mesh from collapsing.
Earap is defined as the as-rigid-as-possible term that encourages the 3D mesh to be more rigid, while Enorm is the normal consistency regularizer defined on the posed mesh.
Earap distributes changes in length among multiple edges with respect to the MANO template.
The appearance term Eapp measures the similarity between the input and the rendered image, and is affected by the geometry change as the texture is mapped to the triangle mesh for rendering.
Eapp is defined as the combination of Ephoto, Evgg, and Eapp reg, where Ephoto is a per-pixel color difference between input and predicted images.
Eapp includes Evgg loss that captures the perceptual difference between the two images by comparing features extracted using the VGG model.
Eapp regularization term encourages both albedo and normal map to be locally smooth.
Eapp regularization term encourages both albedo and normal map to be locally smooth.
